# YggMesh
> YggMesh is open-source OpenWrt firmware that turns low-cost travel routers into a self-organizing, zero-configuration, end-to-end encrypted off-grid mesh network. Nodes discover each other automatically and start routing the moment they power on. The moment at least one node gets internet access, the whole network does too.

YggMesh combines three layers:
- **802.11s WiFi mesh**: Routers form a wireless backhaul using the 802.11s protocol with SAE encryption. The 802.11s interfaces are used directly as the transport — no L2 routing daemon is involved.
- **Yggdrasil**: An end-to-end encrypted L3 overlay network running directly on the 802.11s interfaces. Every node gets a permanent `200::/7` IPv6 address derived from its public key. Nodes discover each other via multicast — no static configuration, no central registry, no internet required.
- **WiFi hotspot**: Each node creates a public, open WiFi access point (`YggMesh`) with 802.11r/k/v seamless roaming. Connected clients receive a `200::/7` Yggdrasil IPv6 address via SLAAC.

Important notes:
- The project is at an extremely early stage; things may break. Use at your own risk.
- Firmware is designed for fresh (virgin) installations only. OTA upgrades via `sysupgrade` or LuCI are not supported and may wipe a node's cryptographic identity and configuration.
- Clearnet (internet) access is NOT provided out-of-the-box after flashing. Configuration after first boot is required.
- All nodes ship with the default root password `yggmesh`. Change it after first boot.
- Alfis DNS forwarding lets WiFi clients resolve `.ygg` and other Yggdrasil domains out of the box.

## Documentation
- [README](https://github.com/ed-asriyan/YggMesh/blob/master/README.md): Full project overview, deployment, building, verification, and clearnet access instructions.
- [Website](https://yggmesh.com/): Landing page with use-case scenarios and a high-level explanation of how it works. Also available within Yggdrasil at [yggmesh.ygg](http://yggmesh.ygg).

## Use Cases
- Disaster/outage resilience: when cellular networks go down in a city, YggMesh routers (running off power banks or plugged in at cafes, shops, and homes) find each other and form a shared network; any node with fiber brings the whole mesh online.
- Cut-off communities: a town relying mostly on cellular LTE stays connected when service drops — neighbors' devices mesh together automatically, and the few homes with fiber share their uplink village-wide.
- Off-grid groups: camps, expeditions, and events get an instant local network with no accounts, passwords, or setup; a single satellite/uplink connection on one node is shared instantly with everyone.

## Deployment
- [Releases](https://github.com/ed-asriyan/YggMesh/releases): Download prebuilt firmware for supported devices.

Supported devices (`device_keyname`): GL.iNet GL-AXT1800 / Slate AX (`axt1800`), GL.iNet GL-MT3000 / Beryl AX (`mt3000`), GL.iNet GL-MT6000 / Flint 2 (`mt6000`), Asus RT-AX53U (`ax53u`), GL.iNet GL-AR300M16 (`ar300m16`), TP-Link CPE710 v1 (`cpe710`), Cudy AP3000 Outdoor V1 (`ap3000outdoor`).

Deployment steps: download or build firmware, flash via LuCI (System → Backup/Flash Firmware) or `sysupgrade -n -v /tmp/openwrt-*-sysupgrade.bin` (do not keep settings), wait ~30s extra on first boot for self-configuration, then repeat for every router. No per-node configuration is needed — all nodes are identical.

## Building
- [GitHub Actions build](https://github.com/ed-asriyan/YggMesh/actions): Fork the repo, run the "Build Firmware" workflow with device inputs (`device`, `default_root_password`, `private_ssid`, `yggdrasil_dns`, `yggdrasil_peers`). Push a git tag (e.g. `v1.0.0`) to trigger a release build for all devices.
- Local build: Requires Linux with `wget`, `zstd`, `make`, `python3` (or the repo devcontainer). Set `MESH_ID`, `MESH_KEY`, `YGG_PORT`, `PRIVATE_SSID`, `DEFAULT_ROOT_PASSWORD`, `YGGDRASIL_DNS`, `YGGDRASIL_PEERS` and run `./scripts/build.sh <device_keyname>`. The OpenWrt Image Builder (~1.5 GB per target) is downloaded on first run; output lands in `output/`.

Shared mesh credentials (`MESH_ID=yggmesh/mesh`, `MESH_KEY=qJ7tN2vL8pR4xKcM`, `YGG_PORT=17000`) are recommended defaults so all YggMesh forks/deployments can peer with each other. Change them only to create a fully isolated mesh. `MESH_KEY` is not a cryptographic secret — it is baked into the firmware image.

## Clearnet Access
To reach the internet, a YggMesh node must be configured (after first boot) with global Yggdrasil peers and/or a Yggdrasil-to-clearnet proxy. Nothing is hardcoded in the firmware.
- [Public Yggdrasil peers](https://publicpeers.neilalexander.dev): Add a few geographically close peer URIs via LuCI (yggdrasil interface → Peers) to connect the local mesh to the global Yggdrasil network.
- [yggdrasil-clearnet-proxy](https://github.com/ed-asriyan/yggdrasil-clearnet-proxy): Set up a SOCKS5 proxy to reach the public internet from devices on the YggMesh WiFi.
- [Yggdrasil services](https://yggdrasil-network.github.io/services.html): Public Yggdrasil websites useful for verifying connectivity.

## Optional
- [Yggdrasil Network](https://yggdrasil-network.github.io): The end-to-end encrypted IPv6 overlay routing protocol used by YggMesh.
- [IEEE 802.11s](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IEEE_802.11s): The WiFi mesh standard used for the wireless backhaul.
- [Alfis](https://alfis.name): Decentralized DNS used for resolving `.ygg` and related domains.
- [Contact](mailto:yggmesh@asriyan.me): Email for contributions, feedback, and hardware support. See also [asriyan.me](https://asriyan.me) or [asriyan.ygg](http://asriyan.ygg).
- [License: MIT](https://github.com/ed-asriyan/YggMesh/blob/master/LICENSE)
